title: Prisoner of the state / Guo jia de qiu tu : Zhao Ziyang de mi mi lu yin
originalTitle: 國家的囚徒 : 趙紫陽的秘密錄音
description: Prisoner of the state / Guo jia de qiu tu : Zhao Ziyang de mi mi lu yin is a book by Ziyang Zhao. The source page records removal or withholding in school libraries amid the post-2020 tightening of Hong Kong's public reading infrastructure.
descriptionParagraphs: Prisoner of the state / Guo jia de qiu tu : Zhao Ziyang de mi mi lu yin is a book by Ziyang Zhao. The source page records removal or withholding in school libraries amid the post-2020 tightening of Hong Kong's public reading infrastructure.

What makes it interesting is the prison-censorship logic: officials treat the book as a practical threat model and collapse the distinction between reading about something and doing it. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Hong Kong. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The source page records removal or withholding in school libraries amid the post-2020 tightening of Hong Kong's public reading infrastructure. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Prisoner of the state / Guo jia de qiu tu : Zhao Ziyang de mi mi lu yin is a book by Ziyang Zhao. The source page records removal or withholding in school libraries amid the post-2020 tightening of Hong Kong's public reading infrastructure.
banningParagraphs: Prisoner of the state / Guo jia de qiu tu : Zhao Ziyang de mi mi lu yin entered censorship debates as a book associated with circulation politics, institutional control, and risk knowledge. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around instructional harm, national security, political dissent, and public order.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2022-06-22 in Hong Kong, where Hong Kong public libraries and other government-managed collections removed from government-managed collections. The source page records removal or withholding in school libraries amid the post-2020 tightening of Hong Kong's public reading infrastructure. counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
